features.summary: NVIDIA reported Q2 fiscal 2023 revenue of $6.7 billion, down 19% sequentially and up 3% year-on-year, missing the $8.1 billion outlook. Gaming revenue fell sharply by 44% sequentially and 33% year-on-year due to macroeconomic headwinds and reduced consumer demand, particularly in Europe and China. Data Center revenue grew 1% sequentially and 61% year-on-year, driven by strong demand from hyperscale customers, while Automotive revenue increased 59% sequentially and 45% year-on-year.
features.management_outlook: For Q3, NVIDIA expects revenue to decline sequentially in Gaming and Professional Visualization due to inventory corrections, while anticipating growth in Data Center and Automotive segments. The revenue guidance is set at approximately $5.9 billion, with gross margins expected to be around 62.4% GAAP and 65% non-GAAP. Management remains optimistic about long-term growth drivers in Gaming and the upcoming product launches.
features.risks: NVIDIA faces significant risks from macroeconomic conditions impacting consumer demand, particularly in the Gaming segment. Additionally, supply chain disruptions have affected product availability, and there is uncertainty regarding the performance of the Chinese hyperscale market. The company has also taken substantial inventory write-downs, reflecting a need to adjust to changing demand expectations.
features.key_drivers: Key growth drivers include the ongoing demand for AI solutions in the Data Center and Automotive sectors, with NVIDIA's Hopper architecture expected to enhance performance in large language models and other AI applications. The company is also focused on expanding its Omniverse platform and leveraging its leadership in 3D graphics and gaming technology to capture new market opportunities.
